observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the management direct the following
officers to:
The Municipal Accountant:
a) Prepare an adjusting entry to derecognize the cost of the issued tangible items
previously classified as PPE with amounts below the capitalization threshold, to
conform with the retrospective application of the change in accounting policy as
prescribed under COA Circular No. 2022-004; and
b) Include disclosures of the Local Road Network System in the Notes to Financial
Statements based on the prescribed format.
The Municipal General Services Officer:
c) Conduct a complete inventory taking of all properties in the ensuing years and
submit the required Report on Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment
(RPCPPE) every January 31 of each year to the Office of the Auditor;
d) Coordinate with the Accounting Office to fast-track the reconciliation of the
inventory reports and accounting records; and
e) Cancel the Inventory Custodian Slips of all the previously issued low-valued
tangible items previously classified as PPE with expired useful life as of
December 31, 2024, including the extinguishment of the related property
accountabilities.
We further recommended that management expedite the implementation of the “One-
Time Cleansing” of PPE account balances in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-
006 dated January 31, 2020.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended to the Management the following courses
of action:
a. Review the CY 2023 and CY 2024 property taxes and recognize the corresponding
Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax Receivables;
b. Strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2023-008 on the required
analysis of dormant accounts by performing the regular and periodic verification,
analysis, and validation of the existence of the receivables and determine the
concerned debtors and accountable officers for strict monitoring and disposition
of said accounts; and
c. Evaluate the Other Receivables and Due from NGOs/POs accounts and validate
their existence and collectability. Implement appropriate measures in compliance
with IPSAS 41, particularly in recognizing an allowance for impairment losses, if
necessary, to ensure accurate financial reporting and prevent the overstatement
of receivables in the financial statements.

In view of the foregoing observation, we recommended and the Management agreed
to take up the unrecorded bank account in the books of accounts and regularly
monitor and reconcile the status of all the bank accounts maintained by the
Municipality and prepare pertinent adjustments, if warranted.

We recommended the following courses of action:
To the Inventory Committee:
1. Strictly adhere and comply to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 which provides for the Guidelines and Procedures in the Conduct of Physical Count of PPEs in accordance with the prescribes for the Guidelines and Procedures in the Conduct of Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE), Recognition of PPE Items Found at Station, and Disposition for Non-Existing/Missing PPE Items, for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances for Government Agencies
To the Municipal Accountant:
2. Maintain PPE Ledger Cards to record the custody, estimated useful life, depreciation, impairment and disposal of those assets; 
3. Exert more effort to come up with a more reliable records and scrutinize and investigate the accounts with “for verification” status; 
4. Recognize in the books of accounts all completed Road Network Assets and duly owned parcels of Land on cost or fair value, whichever is applicable.
5. Provide the depreciation expenses for all depreciable properties and recognize it in the books of accounts by preparing an adjusting journal entry. In case of absence of records as basis for the computation, assess whether there is indication for impairment and recognize impairment loss, if warranted. After the recognition of impairment loss, compute the depreciation charge for the properties using the revised carrying amount, less its residual value, on a systematic basis over its remaining useful life or revised estimated useful life.
6. Ensure that the accounting procedures are done in a timely manner, through proper time management and sound internal control, in order to achieve a fair presentation of financial statements.
To the Municipal General Services Officer and Inventory Committee:
7. Conduct complete physical count of all LGU properties together with other members of the Inventory Committee of the LGU and report the same using the prescribed RPCPPE form in NGAS Manual for LGUs and ensure that all the details required in RPCPPE are filled out to ensure proper reconciliation with the accounting records.
8. Maintain proper and complete records of the PPEs, particularly the stock cards and property cards as stipulated under the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume
9. Ensure that any information included in the report presents fair and accurate data that is reliable and verifiable against supporting documents.
We further recommended that management expedite the implementation of the “One-Time Cleansing” of PPE account balances in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.
observations.managementAction:
The MGSO acknowledged the recommendations and explained that the physical inventory count of PPE items had already been fully conducted. The RPCPPE was submitted to the Audit Team on February 28, 2025. Furthermore, they have committed to conducting timely inventory counts in the coming years and ensuring prompt submission of required reports. Additionally, the Municipal Accountant positively accepted the recommendations and ensured to make all necessary adjustments in the books. He also committed in intensifying efforts to trace PPE items with unknown whereabouts and to improve overall asset management.
Moreover, the management is trying their best to expedite the availment of the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts. They are also exploring additional measures to strengthen internal controls and enhance the accuracy of financial records related to PPE.
observations.reason: The Inventory taking has already completed and reconstitute corresponding composition of the Committee: The RPCPPE not submitted within the prescribed period, due to demanding activities of the Office of the Municipality. Moreover, the Municipal Accountant is trying its best in identifying the PPE items marked as "for verification". Hence, this was reiterated and incorporated in Part II of this AAR.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following courses of action:
1. Strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 on the required analysis of dormant accounts by performing the regular and periodic verification, analysis and validation of the existence of the receivables for strict monitoring and disposition of said accounts and for possible request for the write off of the specified dormant receivables to the Commission on Audit.
2. Evaluate the Other Receivables and Due from NGOs/POs accounts and validate their existence and collectability. Effect appropriate measures in accordance with IPSAS 29 and consequently prevent the overstatement of the Receivable account in the financial statements.
3. Follow the guidelines set in Section 7 of the above-mentioned Circular to come up with the correct valuation of receivable accounts.
observations.managementAction: The Management committed to comply with the recommendations accordingly and will implement necessary measures to ensure compliance and improve financial management practices. Additionally, the Municipal Accountant assured to thoroughly review the accounts, identifying those requiring adjustments, and making appropriate estimates for any potential impairment. Furthermore, they ensured to enhance monitoring mechanisms to prevent future discrepancies and ensure the accurate valuation of assets.
observations.reason: There were still balances subject for verification which were already inactive in nature.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following:
1. Direct the LDRRM Council, through the LDRRM officer, to prepare a complete and comprehensive LDRRMFIP which include both the current appropriations and the unexpended prior year balances.
2. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to record the reclassification entry to reflect the accurate balance of the Trust Liabilities-DRRMF and the Other Payables accounts in the books.
3. Ensure that the PPAs funded by previous years’ unexpended balances in the Special Trust Fund, are clearly reflected in the Annual Investment Plan prior to its release and utilization.
4. Ensure that the preparation of the LDRRMF Investment Plan complies with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002, and accurately includes all projects and activities funded by the unexpended balances from previous years.
observations.managementAction: As of this writing, the Municipal Accountant has already made the necessary adjusting entry to transfer the unexpended balances of the LDRRMF to the appropriate account, “Trust Liability-DRRM”. Furthermore, he committed to ensuring full transparency by disclosing the details of the LDRRMF in the Notes to Financial Statements, following the prescribed format. In addition, the Municipal DRRM Officer ensured to incorporate in the LDRRMFIP for the succeeding years the Programs, Projects, and Activities (PPAs) that will be charged against the Trust Liability – DRRM under the Special Trust Fund.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following:
1. Address the causes of delays and ensure the efficient implementation of all priority development programs and projects under the 20% LDF that are envisioned to help in the attainment of social, economic, and environmental development of the LGU and directly supportive of the Philippine Development Plan and Public Investment Program.
2. Revisit the AIP for the year as well as the continuing appropriations for projects of prior years and reevaluate the relevance of said projects to the present priority needs of the municipality.
3. To the Local Chief Executive to require the offices/officials concerned to identify the gaps in the determination and implementation of development projects thereby come up with corrective measures to ensure optimal utilization of funds and timely delivery of value-added services and benefits to constituents.
4. To the Local Development Council and Local Finance Committee to assign specific timelines for the identified development projects to evaluate achievement rate and identify areas with major shortfall or needs improvement.
5. To the Municipal Engineering Office to submit status report of the above-mentioned continuing programs, projects and activities including the plans and actions for the pending/unimplemented programs in accordance with Section 2.1 of COA Circular 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013.
observations.managementAction: The Management has already provided the list of PPAs that have been physically completed as of this writing. For projects that remained unfinished, they committed to expediting the implementation of those that are still relevant and reprogramming those deemed no longer feasible. This ensures that resources are optimally utilized and aligned with current priorities. Furthermore, the Management assured that the As-Built Plans for identified projects with discrepancies will be submitted as soon as they become available.
observations.reason: There were projects that need to be verified further, whether some programs and projects need to be reprogrammed. Hence, this was reiterated and incorporated in Part II of this AAR.

observations.managementAction: The Management committed to comply with the recommendations and will ensure that responsible offices maintain stock cards and properly monitor and update the stock cards. They will require supporting documents such as RIS and ensure timely submission to the Municipal Accountant.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended the following to the Management, with the
Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and the Municipal Engineer as
leads:
a. Address the causes of delays and ensure the efficient implementation of all
priority development programs and projects under the 20% LDF that are
envisioned to help in the attainment of social, economic, and environmental
development of the LGU and directly supportive of the Philippine Development
Plan and Public Investment Program; and
b. Revisit the AIP for the year as well as the continuing appropriations for projects of
prior years and reevaluate the relevance of said projects to the present priority
needs of the municipality.

We recommended the following courses of actions; a) To the Local Chief Executive: require the offices/officials concerned to identify the gaps in the determination and implementation of development projects thereby come up with corrective measures to ensure optimal utilization of funds and timely delivery of value-added services and benefits to constituents; b) To the Local Development Council and Local Finance Committee to assign specific timelines for the identified development projects to evaluate achievement rate and identify areas with major shortfall or needs improvement in order to make timely and periodic remedial measures and determine the feasibility of implementation of prior year’s unimplemented projects; and c) To the Municipal Engineering Office to submit status report of the above-mentioned continuing programs, projects and activities including the plans and actions for the pending/unimplemented programs in accordance with Section 2.1 of COA Circular 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended and Management agreed on
the following courses of action to be taken by the agency head, viz:
a) Instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain and update the PPE ledger cards
and the GSO of its property cards as control records to facilitate the regular
reconciliation of records and conduct further investigation into the circumstances
surrounding the loss of some items that were not found during the actual count. If
still unlocated, determine the concerned personnel liable for the loss and require
them to pay for the cost of the property since the required request for
accountability has not been filed within the prescribed period;
b) Ensure that all insurable physical assets are adequately and reliably protected
against any damage to or loss of properties pursuant to Republic Act No. 656;
c) Give instruction to the Municipal Treasurer and/or Municipal Assessor to fast-
track the preparation of documentary requirements to secure the Transfer
Certificates of Title (TCT) under the name of the Municipality of M’lang for all
parcels of land to support claims of ownership and provide copies to the audit
team; and also trace records/documents to establish ownership of the parcels of
land with incomplete data as to acquisition date and cost and if found to be legally
owned by the Municipality, expedite also the application for the land titles under
the name of the LGU to avoid loss of properties in case other interested parties
may claim the same in the future;
d) Require the Municipal Accountant to recognize in the books of accounts all
completed Road Network Assets and duly owned parcels of Land on cost or fair
value whichever is applicable and direct the Municipal Accountant, the MGSO, the
Municipal Engineer and the Inventory Committee to strictly perform their duties
and responsibilities in the accounting and reporting of Local Road Network as
mandated under COA Circular No. 2015-008 and submit the pertinent reports as
prescribed by existing laws, rules and regulations; and
e) Direct the Inventory Committee to strictly adhere and comply to the provisions of
COA Circular 2020-006 which provides for the Guidelines and Procedures in the
Conduct of Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE), Recognition
of PPE Items Found at Station, and Disposition for Non-existing/Missing PPE
Items, for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances of Government
Agencies and to conduct a complete inventory taking and submit the Report on
Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) every January 31 of
each year to the Office of the Auditor.

To further improve the reliability of financial reporting, we recommended and
management agreed that the Municipal Accountant:
a) Record and adjust the accounts affected in order to put the intra-agency accounts
in agreement considering the noted variances as of December 31, 2021;
b) Henceforth, simultaneously record transactions relating to the reciprocal
accounts; and
c) Ensure regular reconciliation of reciprocal accounts at least once a year for better
and fair presentation of financial statements.

We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive:
a) Require the responsible offices entrusted with inventory items to judiciously
maintain stock cards and properly monitor and update the stock cards to generate
a more reliable inventory report;
b) Ensure that all issuances of inventory items be supported with Requisition and
Issue Slip (RIS) to support the issuance or distribution of inventory items and
regularly submit to the Municipal Accountant for prompt recording;
c) Necessitate the Municipal Accountant to institute necessary adjustments in
relation to the Inventory account and maintain inventory ledger cards for separate
inventory items and promptly update the ledger cards as part of its internal
control and timely reconciliation; and
d) Review and reassess the accounting policy and procedure of the LGU for
inventory undertakings pursuant to existing laws, rules and regulations taking in to
consideration the soundness of the internal control adopted by the LGU.

We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive:
a) Direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to set a period and
timeline to work-back, reconcile and determine further the causes of the
discrepancies and to perform monthly reconciliation of the cash accounts
following the prescribed rules and procedures of COA Circular 97-002 to avoid the
accumulation of unreconciled items in the books and make appropriate valid
adjustments on the books that were found to be misstated;
b) Reconsider the efficiency and effectiveness of the internal control specifically the
collection procedures of the LGU from receipt, recording, monitoring and
reconciliation and come up with a more operative and dependable controls over
cash; and
c) Comply strictly with the provision of COA Circular 97-002 to avoid the same
lapses in the succeeding transactions.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended and management agreed on
the following courses of action:
To Municipal Accountant:
a) Prepare an adjusting journal entry to recognize all the valid reconciling items and
effect the necessary entries on the restoration of cash of the stale checks;
b) Furnish immediately the bank of the Bank Reconciliation Statement and confer
with the concerned bank personnel all the noted bank reconciling items.
c) Ensure the complete preparation of bank reconciliation statements on a monthly
basis for all cash in bank accounts maintained by the Municipality.
To Municipal Treasurer:
d) Regularly monitor the status of checks released and unreleased. Send written
notice to the concerned payee regarding the existence of the checks at least a
month before it becomes stale in compliance with Section 59 of Volume I of the
Manual on NGAS;
e) Return the original DVs and supporting documents of the staled checks to the
Municipal Accountant who will prepare a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record
the transaction as Accounts Payable;
To Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer:
f) Make a follow up with the concerned bank personnel on the copy of bank
statement and/or secure copy of snapshot of the account for a certain period if the
bank statement is not yet available; and
g) Perform a regular reconciliation of the accounts to avoid the accumulation of
unreconciled items in the books.

We recommended and management agreed to:
a) Direct the concerned officials to immediately settle the unliquidated cash
advance. Non-compliance will constitute a valid cause for the withholding of
his/her salary and the instruction of other sanctions as provided in COA Circular
No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997;
b) Enforce strictly the provision of Section 89 of P.D. 1445 and require the concerned
officials and employees with unliquidated cash advances to immediately settle the
same; and
c) Require the Municipal Accountant to send demand letters to the aforementioned
accountable officers, and provide a copy of the demand letters to the Audit Team
as confirmation of the Municipal Accountant's action to demand
liquidation/settlement.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended and management agreed on the following
courses of actions:
a) To the Local Chief Executive to require the offices/officials concerned to identify
the gaps in the determination and implementation of development projects
thereby come up with corrective measures to ensure optimal utilization of funds
and timely delivery of value-added services and benefits to constituents;
b) To the Local Development Council and Local Finance Committee to assign
specific timelines for the identified development projects to evaluate achievement
rate and identify areas with major shortfall or needs improvement in order to make
timely and periodic remedial measures and determine the feasibility of
implementation of prior year’s unimplemented projects; and
c) To the Municipal Engineering Office to submit status report of the above-
mentioned continuing programs, projects and activities including the plans and
actions for the pending/unimplemented programs in accordance with Section 2.1
of COA Circular 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended and management agreed on
the following, viz:
a) Ascertain that the provisions of Republic Act No. 10121 are implemented
appropriately, particularly the allocation for the LDRRM Fund, which will
strengthen the Municipality's capacity to develop a well-defined and budgeted
LDRRM Plan;
b) Ensure that allotted budget for the LDRRM Fund were properly appropriated for
the year to ascertain that all programs, projects and activities identified in the
LDRRM Fund will be realized accordingly and reported appropriately;
c) Properly implement the LDRRM Plan and optimize the utilization of its LDRRM
Fund to lessen the socioeconomic and environmental impacts of disasters in the
community and disaster risks, and to enhance disaster preparedness and
response capabilities of the community;
d) Include in the plans the utilization of the unexpended LDRRM Fund balances from
prior years, and specify the list of programs, projects, and activities to be charged
against the unexpended LDRRM Fund of previous years in its LDRRM Fund
Investment Plan before utilization of the same pursuant to the provisions of COA
Circular No. 2012-002; and
e) Adhere strictly to the accounting principles outlined in COA Circular 2012-002,
particularly with regard to the usage of appropriate accounts when transferring the
unexpended balance and disclosing the DRRM Fund's appropriation and utilization
on the Notes to Financial Statements.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended and management agreed on
the following, viz:
a) Strictly adhere to Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014 for
the timely submission of the said reports to the COA audit team;
b) Allocate at least 5% of the total annual budget for the implementation of GAD-
related activities as required under Section 36(a) of RA No. 9710 or the Magna
Carta of Women;
c) Require the Heads of implementing departments/offices to ensure that GAD
projects, activities and programs are implemented as planned to attain the GAD
objectives;
d) Maximize the utilization of the GAD funds through the implementation of GAD-
related programs and projects in order to attain the objective for which funds were
provided; and
e) Require the GAD Focal Point System to institutionalize the GAD database with
adequate, updated and systematically gathered sex-disaggregated data which will
provide the bases for gender analysis, planning, programming and policy
formulation and establish a GAD monitoring and evaluation system to come up
with a more GAD responsive and related programs and activities.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended and management agreed on
the following, viz:
a) Strictly adhere to the provision set forth in the DILG MC No. 2019-149 especially
on the prompt and updated posting of the required documents in at least three (3)
conspicuous places;
b) Capacitate the Municipal Planning and Development Office (MPDO) in terms of
manpower, trainings and other resources to provide a more efficient and effective
services with regards to Full Disclosure Policy;
c) Require the MPDO to be prompt and accurate on the posting of the required
documents and instruct the Department Heads of concerned offices for the timely
submission of the reports; and
d) Maximize the utilization of the FDP Board and website of the Municipality.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended and Management agreed on
the following courses of action to be taken by the agency head, viz;
a) Direct the Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that the said remaining balance
were free from any material encumbrances;
b) Direct the Municipal Treasurer to deposit the undisbursed balances of BGCM to
the account of National Treasury; and
c) Upon reversion of the said balance from the books of the agency, assign a focal
person to immediately submit to the DBM a comprehensive report on fund
utilization of its allocation from the BGCM using the prescribed format provided
on the aforementioned guidelines.

We recommended and management agreed to:
a) Strictly monitor and encourage all regular and permanent to maintain the monthly
net take home pay before granting of loans and other borrowings accruing to
them; and
b) Instruct the Accounting Unit and Payroll Officer to adhere strictly to the general
provisions of the General Appropriations Act of each fiscal year to ensure that the
net take home pay of each employee is not below the minimum amount.

We recommended that the Management: a) prepare the pertinent adjusting journal entries to correct erroneous entries made on the receipt and disbursements of funds; b) recognize the unrecorded assets and ensure that the properties are duly accounted in the books; and c) ensure that succeeding transactions be properly recorded to the appropriate accounts.

We recommended that the agency head: a) Direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to work-back to determine further the causes of the discrepancies and to perform monthly reconciliation of the cash accounts following the prescribed rules and procedures of COA Circular 97-002 to avoid the accumulation of unreconciled items in the books and make appropriate valid adjustments on the books that were found to be misstated; b) Review the internal control specifically the collection procedures of the LGU from receipt, recording, monitoring and reconciliation; and c) Comply strictly with the provision of COA Circular 97-002 to avoid the same lapses in the succeeding transactions.

We recommended that the Management: a) Require the responsible offices entrusted with inventory items to judiciously maintain stock cards and properly monitor and update the stock cards to generate a more reliable inventory report; b) Ensure that all issuances of inventory items be supported with Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) and regularly submit to the Municipal Accountant within five days after end of the month pursuant to Section 189 of the Government Accounting Manual for Local Government Units and require the concerned offices to submit liquidation documents to support the issuance or distribution of inventory items; c) Necessitate the Municipal Accountant to institute necessary adjustments in relation to the Inventory account and maintain inventory ledger cards for separate inventory items and promptly update the ledger cards as part of its internal control and timely reconciliation; and d) Review and reassess the accounting policy and procedure of the LGU for inventory undertakings pursuant to Government Accounting Manual for LGUs and other related laws, rules and regulations taking in to consideration the soundness of the internal control adopted by the LGU.

We recommended that the agency head: a) For disbursements for the utilization of the Bayanihan Grant - submit a written justification for processing the transaction and making payment without documentation and require the Accounting Office to submit to the Audit Team all the lacking documents; b) For non-preparation and non-posting of Monthly Report on Fund Utilization and Status of Programs/Project/Activity - require the MPDC to regularly prepare the required reports and post the said reports to at least 3 conspicuous public places; send a written notice to DBM, House of Representatives, Senate of the Philippines, House Committee on Appropriations, Senate Committee on Finance after posting the required reports; and strictly observe the timelines in the posting of the required operational reports at the designated places in order to attain maximum transparency in LGU governance.

We recommended that the LGU: a) Coordinate with the DSWD regularly to enhance or improve the distribution of succeeding subsidy programs; b) Increase their manpower to facilitate the distribution, accomplishment and encoding of SAC forms and the preparation and approval of the payroll; and c) Strictly follow the schedule set to ensure the timely and efficient distribution of cash assistance to the beneficiaries. Also, for repeated availing by same beneficiaries: a) The LGU shall report to the DSWD about the noted duplication of beneficiaries for further validation/investigation and appropriate action; b) The DSWD shall strictly intensify the validation process before and after the payout to avoid double claims or same scenario to happen in the future. Ensure that no payout shall be allowed, unless complete validation process that consists of eligibility and duplication checks are performed by them and by the LGU and other concerned agencies; and c) Compel the duplicate beneficiaries to return the excess SAP benefits to the government; Disqualify them from receiving the next tranche of the cash assistance as emphasized in Item VIII B. (6.2) of DSWD Memo Circular No. 09 series of 2020. Ensure that duplicate beneficiaries are collected and sanctioned.

We recommended that the Management: a) Instruct the Accounting Unit to maintain a separate book for the special trust fund and bank account intended for the reporting and monitoring of the LDRRM fund; b) Strictly comply with the provisions in the COA Circular No. 2012-002 and ensure that allotted budget for the Local DRRM Fund were properly appropriated for the year to ascertain that all programs, projects and activities identified in the Local DRRM Fund will be realized accordingly and reported appropriately; and c) Properly implement the Local DRRM Plan and optimize the utilization of its Local DRRM Fund to lessen the socioeconomic and environmental impacts of disasters in the community and disaster risks, and to enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities of the community.

We recommended that the Management: a) Provide a timeline on the projects, programs and activities that are continuing appropriations and ascertain further that programs, projects and activities should be specifically identified, quantified, implemented, monitored, accomplished and reported in accordance with plans and targets set for the year; and b) Strictly adhere to measures to optimize the utilization of the 20% development fund for desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes. Ensure that overcharging and misclassification will be avoided and that funds will be used for proper development projects.

We recommended that the Management: a) Strictly adhere to Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014 for the timely submission of the said reports to the COA audit team; b) Allocate at least 5% of the total annual budget for the implementation of GAD-related activities as required under Section 36(a) of RA No. 9710 or the Magna Carta of Women; c) Require the Heads of implementing departments/offices to ensure that GAD projects, activities and programs are implemented as planned to attain the GAD objectives; d) Maximize the utilization of the GAD funds through the implementation of GAD-related programs and projects in order to attain the objective for which funds were provided; and e) Require the GAD Focal Point System to institutionalize the GAD database with adequate, updated and systematically gathered sex-disaggregated data which will provide the bases for gender analysis, planning, programming and policy formulation and establish a GAD monitoring and evaluation system to come up with a more GAD responsive and related programs and activities.

We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Accountant to scrutinize the details of the transactions pertaining to inter-fund transfers, reconcile the reciprocal accounts on all funds maintained by the Municipality, prepare reconciliation statements, and effect adjustments to reflect the correct account balances, thus, present reliable data on the Municipality’s financial condition as of a given period of time. Reconciliation shall be made regularly to avoid the accumulation of unreconciled balances of the accounts.

We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Accountant to: a) Exert extra effort to account the said receivables by tracing back into previous years’ files and reports. If after exhausting all possible means and the claims could not be accounted, request for write-off of the above receivables supported by the above-cited documents and schedules, follow the procedures provided under Section 8 of the COA Circular 2016-005; b) Provide for an allowance for impairment for dormant receivable accounts in order to have a fair presentation of the financial statements as required under PPSAS 29; and c) Follow the guidelines set in Section 7 of the above-mentioned Circular to come up with the correct valuation of receivable accounts.

We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Accountant to adjust all book reconciling items and to see to it that all succeeding cash transactions be properly recorded in the books of accounts of the Municipality to come up with a reliable balance of the accounts in the financial statements; coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer and perform a regular reconciliation of the accounts to avoid the accumulation of unreconciled items in the books; and furnish immediately the bank of the Bank Reconciliation Statement and confer with the concerned bank personnel all the noted bank reconciling items.

We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Accountant to transfer all completed projects under the Construction in Progress accounts to their appropriate asset account for a more reliable reporting of the transactions in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the non-current portion of Loans Payable – Domestic for better presentation of liabilities of the LGU in the Statement of Financial Position as at year-end/reporting date. Moreover, provide the necessary information relating to the accounts as herein mentioned and submit it to the Office of the Auditor for its inclusion in the LGU’s Notes to Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Management: a) strictly adhere with the provisions of DILG-DBM Joint Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 in the utilization of the 20% development fund; b) ensure that the fund be fully utilized to attain its desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes in the implementation of projects; c) stop the practice of charging expenses which is administrative in nature and not in the implementation of development projects; and d) utilize the above-mentioned building as a multi-purpose one that will contribute to social development.

These personnel who contracted loans from these lending institutions must directly remit their payments to the collecting agents/office of these creditors.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should maintain a separate book of
accounts for the PhilHealth Capitation Fund for the receipt and utilization of the same to
facilitate timely preparation and submission of the required reports.

We recommended that the Municipal Health Office coordinate with the barangay
health centers and determine their needs that can be funded by the PFPR so that the
government‟s goal of providing preventive care services to indigents will be fully
implemented, and thus, best results could be achieved in the most opportune time. Also,
maximize the use of the funds intended especially for Drugs and Medicines so that
beneficiaries can avail and use of the program.

We recommended to liquidate immediately the unsettled cash advances and strictly
observe the provision set forth in COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommend to the management to immediately return the abovementioned
balances of the fund transfer received from the source agency.

We recommend that management direct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the
reciprocal accounts, prepare reconciliation statement, and effect adjustments to reflect the
correct account balances, thus, present reliable data on the Municipality‟s financial
operation as of a given period of time.

We recommend that management require the Municipal‟s Bids and Awards
Committee (BAC) to prepare the Procurement Monitoring Report to be approved by the
head of the procuring entity and submit the same to the Government Procurement and
Policy Board (GPPB) in printed and electronic format within fourteen (14) calendar days
after the end of each semester.

We recommend to the management to require the Municipal Treasurer and the
Accountant to reconcile their respective records regularly. Prepare and effect the
necessary adjusting entries to finally determine the correct balance of Cash in Vault
account.

Further, comply strictly with the provision of COA Circular 97-002 to avoid the
same lapses in the succeeding transactions.

We recommend that management remind or increase awareness of the different
departments/offices on the necessity of preparing and summarizing issuance of supplies
and materials in their respective offices as directed under Chapter 7 of the NGAS Manual
for Local Government Units.

Likewise, require the observance of the perpetual inventory method of accounting for
the supplies and materials as set forth under Section 4(j) and 114 of the NGAS Manual
prescribed under COA Circular No. 2002-003 dated June 20, 2002.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer shall remit
promptly and intact all taxes withheld by the Municipality to the Bureau of Internal
Revenue in compliance with the BIR regulations.

We recommended to the management to stop the charges made against Economic
Development Fund unless the program or activities partakes the nature of investment or
capital expenditure leading to the attainment of socio-economic development and
environmental management. See to it that guidelines provided by Joint Memorandum
Circular No. 2011-1 of the Department of Interior of Local Government and Department
of Budget and Management be strictly followed.
Enjoin the GSO to regularly monitor the expiration of all motor vehicles registration and its timely renewal/registration to avoid invalidation and fines/penalties for late/non-registration.

We recommended that the management will resort to NFA as primary supplier or source of rice in all its procurements whenever it require emergency purchase. Also, forge with the NFA an appropriate Memorandum of Agreement to facilitate and operationalize the rice procurement arrangement.
observations.managementAction: All rice procurements were sourced from NFA except those items that require emergency purchase.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that management require the Bids and Awards Committee to submit the needed documentary requirements to support the payment of honoraria as basis in evaluating the propriety and validity of the claim. Stop the processing of claims unless the transactions is supported with proper documentations.

Require the GSO to conduct annual physical count of the PPE and submit to the accountant the RPCPPE as basis for reconciliation. Also, instruct the Municipal GSO and Municipal Accountant to reconcile the records with the physical count and appropriate adjustments should be made to account for unserviceable, obsolete or lost properties. Likewise, the GSO and Accounting Office maintain PPE ledger cards to record acquisition, description, and other information about properties.
observations.managementAction: Call the attention of the team to fast track the lists of PPEs of the LGU Mlang.
observations.reason: Updating is now going on for the inventory of PPEs and unserviceable PPEs for disposal.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the local chief will secure a copy of PNDF Volume 1, current edition, and to direct the Municipal Health Officer and other requisitioning officers to refer as their guide and for the requisition of medicines.
observations.managementAction: The concerned department will secure a copy of PNDF Volume 1 and direct requisitioning officers to refer to the PNDF as guide.
observations.reason: The purchase of medicines needed for total public service is needed to support the constituents of Municipality of Mlang.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Accountant should booked up the unutilized balance of the DRRM Fund and at the same time, transfer the unexpended balance of the 30% Quick Response Fund to the Trust Fund books as a Special Trust Fund under account “Trust Liability – DRRMF”.

We recommend that the management discontinue the practice of allowing deductions on monthly payroll other than those mandated by law. These personnel who contracted loans from these lending institutions must directly remit their payments to the collecting agents/office of these creditors.
observations.reason: Reconciliation is now going on.

observations.recommendation: We recommend to the management to require the Accountant to prepare appropriate adjusting/correcting entry to take up the reconciling items in the books of accounts. Also, provide copy of the bank reconciliation statement to the bank manager of the DBP thru the Audit Team Leader and make coordination with the bank of the various bank errors for proper adjustment in the bank records. Also, verify with the bank status of the recorded deposit in transit as shown in the Bank Reconciliation Statement. Update the preparation of the BRS and ensure that all reconciling items are properly and immediately attended.

We recommend that the management exert their best effort to fully accomplish the targeted priority projects both development projects and programs embodied in the Annual Investment Plan pursuant to its mandate. Strictly administer and supervise the implementation of development projects and likewise monitor its implementation. Determine the reason for non-implementation of some projects and evaluate the possibility to make amendments to some projects which are not feasible for implementation.
observations.reason: The physical accomplishments have been completed but the financial accountability is still on process.
